critic_raw: I want to probe the article age feature: 'article age (relative to the query date).' This feature encodes when an article was created relative to when the query was made. In the test period (months 19-24), articles are older (they were created earlier). If the model learns that 'newer articles are more relevant,' it may systematically downrank well-established foundational articles in the test set. Additionally, the 'coverage' secondary metric measures 'percentage of queries for which the model returns candidates (vs. no match in product area/version constraints).' The version applicability filtering means the model may return no candidates for queries about old or unsupported product versions. How does the design handle queries that return no dense embedding candidates?
defender_raw: Round 1: Article age as a feature: this is a domain-relevant signal — in a technology support context, newer articles may address current product versions more accurately. The model learns the relationship between article age and relevance from training data, not from a hardcoded rule. If older articles are systematically more relevant (foundational knowledge), the model would learn this from click data. The temporal split ensures the model is evaluated on the same age distribution shift that would occur in production. On coverage with version constraints: the coverage secondary metric directly measures the no-candidate problem. If coverage is low for old/unsupported versions, this is an honest finding about the deployment scenario. Both models use the same version constraints, so coverage comparisons are valid.

rounds.critic_followup: On article age: the training period is months 1-12, validation 13-18, test 19-24. Articles created in months 13-24 appear in the test and validation sets but not training. The article age feature for these newer articles is outside the training distribution (all training articles were created before month 13). Does this create an age-based distribution shift?
rounds.defender_followup: Article age is a continuous feature computed as 'relative to the query date' — it is the age of the article at the time of the query, not the article's absolute creation date. An article created in month 15 evaluated in month 20 has age 5 months. The distribution of article ages seen in training (articles 1-12 months old relative to queries in months 1-12) is similar to the distribution in test (articles 1-24 months old relative to queries in months 19-24). Newer articles in test may be younger (recently created), but this is realistic distribution shift that both models face equally. The model's learned age-relevance relationship from training would generalize to new age ranges.

rounds.critic_followup: On the coverage metric: if the dense embedding model has lower coverage than BM25 (more queries return no candidates due to version constraints), this is a deployment concern. How does the comparison handle queries where one model returns candidates but the other doesn't?
rounds.defender_followup: Both models use the same version constraints for filtering (metadata filtering by product area and version). The version filtering is applied as a post-retrieval step to both BM25 and dense retrieval candidates. Coverage differences between the models would reflect differences in the candidate sets generated before version filtering, not differences in filtering rules. If the dense model retrieves fewer version-compatible candidates, this is a genuine retrieval quality difference that the coverage metric correctly captures. The NDCG@5 computation is limited to queries where both models return candidates — or separately analyzed for zero-candidate queries. This is an honest comparison.

adjudication_raw: Two rounds addressing article age distribution shift and coverage comparison for queries with no candidates. Both resolved: article age is relative and generalizes; version filtering applies to both models equally; coverage metric captures zero-candidate problem. Verdict: defense_wins.
